Abu Dhabi National Intelligence Platform

Daily Brief For You!

Abu Dhabi's economy remains on a solid growth trajectory entering Q2 2026. GDP expanded 3.4% YoY with non-oil sectors now contributing 56.3% — a structural milestone ahead of Vision 2030 targets. Inflation held at 2.8%, though food prices signal a +0.4pp risk. Employment hit a 5-year high, while Emiratisation tracking 0.6pp below annual target requires sector-specific intervention. FDI inflows delivered a standout quarter, surging +11.2% to AED 47.8B — the strongest Q1 performance in six years — underscoring sustained investor confidence in Abu Dhabi's diversification story. However, inflation running 40bps above the 2.4% target, largely driven by food price pressures, warrants close monitoring as it could weigh on household consumption and real wage growth in H2. Closing the Emiratisation gap and anchoring inflation will be the twin policy priorities that determine whether Q2 builds on this momentum or signals a plateauing cycle.

AI Insight
Leader trajectory
Abu Dhabi climbed steadily from 2.6% → 3.4% over 4 quarters — the steepest upward run in the GCC.
Rising challenger
Bahrain has accelerated sharply since Q4 2025, closing the gap from −1.0pp to −0.5pp vs Abu Dhabi.
Divergence risk
Qatar and Kuwait are the only two economies trending below GCC average. Qatar has softened for 2 consecutive quarters.
Signal
Kuwait has stayed below GCC average for all 4 quarters with zero QoQ change — a structural review is warranted.
Saudi Arabia
CPI · Inflation management
High relevance
What they did

Introduced a strategic food reserve programme covering 6 months of essential staples, combined with a pre-authorised import duty trigger when global freight index exceeds +15%.

Result

Food CPI held at +1.8% vs Abu Dhabi's +2.4% during the Red Sea disruption — a 0.6pp advantage.

Abu Dhabi takeaway

Extending strategic food reserves to 6 months and introducing a pre-authorised duty trigger could reduce CPI vulnerability during future freight shocks.

Saudi Arabia
Labour · Nationalisation
High relevance
What they did

Introduced mandatory Saudization quotas in retail, hospitality and F&B — combined with a 2-year wage subsidy for each new Saudi national hire in private services.

Result

Saudization rate in private services closed from −5.8pp to −1.9pp below target in 12 months.

Abu Dhabi takeaway

Sector-specific quotas plus time-limited wage subsidy could close Abu Dhabi's Emiratisation gap in private services within 3–4 quarters.

Qatar
FDI · Investment attraction
Medium relevance
What they did

Launched fast-track residency for foreign investors committing QAR 5M+ to non-oil sectors, with a 10-year tax holiday for qualifying businesses in tech, health and education.

Result

Non-oil FDI inflows increased 14% YoY in 2025, with tech sector FDI up 28%.

Abu Dhabi takeaway

A residency-linked incentive anchored to advanced manufacturing and AI investment could strengthen Abu Dhabi's GCC lead in non-oil FDI.

What is a workspace?

A workspace is your personalised view of Bayaan — scoped to a specific location, domain set, and persona. Each workspace independently remembers your pinned indicators, alert configurations, dashboard layouts, and exploration history. Switch freely between workspaces without losing context.

Please write a strategy to increase GDP by leveraging tourism, and include a benchmark comparison with other countries.

Question 1/7

Spain

Thailand
All three
+ Specify Other
Skip
Plan
PLAN PREVIEW
4 sub-questions · review and approve
1
How have Spain, France, and Thailand increased their tourism GDP contribution over the past five years, and what growth trends have been observed?
2
What specific policies, investments, or tactical initiatives have proven most effective in Spain, France, and Thailand for increasing GDP through tourism since 2020?
3
Are there notable differences in the definition or methodology of calculating tourism's GDP contribution between Abu Dhabi and benchmark countries that might affect direct comparison?
4
Based on the above analyses, what are the most applicable and tactical strategies Abu Dhabi could implement to increase GDP through tourism in the coming years?
Approval
Research
Fetching Spain, France, Thailand tourism data…
Querying GDP contribution benchmarks…
Analysing policy effectiveness 2020–2024…
Synthesising Abu Dhabi strategy…
Synthesise
Done

The UAE's unemployment rate stands at 2.7% as of 2024, making it one of the lowest in the Middle East and reflecting a 0.3 percentage point decrease. This improvement indicates a continued confidence and sectoral expansion.

  • The total labor force is estimated to be approximately 6.2 million, with continued growth.
  • The services sector is the largest employer, encompassing tourism, retail, and financial services.
  • The construction sector follows at 17.8%, driven by major infrastructure projects.
  • Other growing sectors include logistics, technology, and economic diversification.
  • Government labor reforms and talent attraction policies have helped maintain high employment levels.

These figures highlight a stable and competitive labor market, with the UAE continuing to attract both regional and international talent.

Labour Anomaly Sentinel

3 anomalies detected — Today's run
06:02 GST
I scanned 1,247,302 records from the Labour Registry and found 3 data points exceeding the σ > 2.0 threshold. The most significant anomaly is in the Construction sector where daily registrations dropped 34% from the 90-day baseline.

Data quality

Today's run completed cleanly. All 1,247,302 rows from labour_db.registrations were cleansed and loaded into ClickHouse · dwh.labour_clean.

You can ask me about data quality, request a sample output, check schema differences, or adjust the transform rules for the next run.

Why were 340 rows skipped on May 23?

I checked the May 23 run log. The 340 skipped rows all failed the null validation rule on the nationality_code field — they came in as empty strings rather than NULL, which your current filter doesn't catch.

I can fix this in two ways:

  • Auto-fix: Update the cleanse rule to treat empty strings as NULL and forward-fill from prior record
  • Quarantine: Route these rows to a labour_rejected table for manual review

Which approach do you prefer?

Abu Dhabi Economy Deep Dive · Ep. 12
Generated from 2 sources · 8 min 42 sec
3:02 / 8:42
Generating podcast…
Creating two-host script from 2 sources
Transcript
Layla: Welcome to the Bayaan Intelligence Podcast. Today we're examining Abu Dhabi's Q1 2026 economic performance — and the headline number is striking.
Khalid: That's right. A 3.4% GDP growth rate — but the real story is the structural shift. Non-oil sectors now account for 56.3% of GDP for the first time.
Layla: A Vision 2030 milestone. And alongside that, FDI inflows hit a six-year high — AED 47.8 billion in a single quarter.
Khalid: Tourism grew 11.4%. The infrastructure investment is paying off. But there's a shadow — Emiratisation tracking 0.6 percentage points below target…
Layla: Which brings us to the CPI picture. Inflation at 2.8% — above the 2.4% policy target for the third consecutive month, driven by food prices.
Khalid: Our recommendation for the next quarter — targeted subsidies on the essential food basket could offset 60% of household impact at just AED 1.2 billion cost.

Home › Observatory › Consumer Price Index

Consumer Price Index — Briefing Desk

Abu Dhabi · March 2026 data · Methodology under active refinement

Home Search results

Search Results

AI Insight

Abu Dhabi's GDP recorded strong growth in 2025, with Q3 2025 reaching AED 325.7 billion, driven by a 7.6% surge in the non-oil economy. As the UAE's largest emirate, it accounts for over 62% of the national GDP, with non-oil activities making up over 54% of its total economic output, highlighting a successful diversification strategy, particularly in manufacturing, construction, and financial services.

Abu Dhabi Department of Economic Development (ADDED) +4

Key GDP Data (2025–2026):

  • Q3 2025 Total GDP: AED 325.7 billion (7.7% growth).
  • Q3 2025 Non-Oil Growth: 7.6% year-on-year.
  • Q2 2025 Total GDP: AED 306.3 billion (3.8% increase).

Key Economic Drivers & Trends:

  • Non-Oil Sector Growth: The non-oil economy is growing rapidly, with significant contributions from manufacturing, financial services, and real estate.
  • Oil Sector: While diversification is a priority, the oil sector remains a significant part of the economy.
  • Key Growth Sectors: Electricity, gas, and water supply showed high growth (16.2%), alongside manufacturing.
  • Growth Outlook: The economy is anticipated to continue growing, supported by industrial transformation and long-term investment strategies.
  • Economic Vision: Abu Dhabi is actively reducing its economic reliance on oil through "Economic Diversification 2.0" strategies.

Home › Observatory › Crisis Intelligence

Overview

Current signals indicate generally stable conditions across most domains, while select financial indicators show emerging pressure. In particular, interbank lending rates have increased above baseline levels, potentially reflecting early signs of tightening liquidity conditions in regional markets. Continued monitoring of financial stress indicators is recommended to detect any escalation in systemic risk.

Home › Economy › Prices › Consumer price index by main expenditure group
Consumer price index by main expenditure group
Measures the weighted average change in prices paid by urban households for housing, water, electricity, gas, and other fuels in Abu Dhabi. Reflects cost of shelter relative to the base period (2014 = 100).

💡 Nov 2024: Water tariff adjustment +0.12bps 💡 Q2 2025: Rental escalation — Khalidiyah 💡 Jan 2025: Status escalated to AT RISK
AI Contextual Intelligence Apr 2025
Drivers
Rental price escalation in Khalidiyah and Al Raha Beach accounts for 61% of monthly variance. Water tariff adjustment added +0.12bps structural floor.
Explore with AI →
Risks
AT RISK for 3 consecutive months. Housing inflation 1.4pp above GCC median — widest gap since Q3 2022. Rent freeze expiration risk Q3 2025.
Assess with AI →
Signals
Electricity sub-index held flat (0.00% MoM). Gas & fuel component decelerating. Al Ain deflation −0.3% is a positive leading signal for outer districts.

  • Peer comparison with GCC housing CPI available — Abu Dhabi leads by 1.4pp.
